text: DASS, or DAUSE, an island in the Persian gulf, in N lat. 25° 10′, E long. 33° 5′. It is nearly 6 m. in length, and of moderate elevation.
DASSAU, or Dassow, a town of Mecklenburg- Schwerin, on a small lake of the same name, at the mouth of the Stepenitz, 11 m. W of Wismar. Pop. 1,792. DAU
DASSEL, a town of Hanover, in the principality and 22 m. NNW of Gottingen, at the confluence of the Ilme and Rothe-Wasser. Pop. 1,629. Linen and paper are manufactured here.
DASSET (Avon), a parish in Warwickshire, 54 m. ESE of Kington. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. 287.
DASSGONG, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, on the Saotri, in N lat. 18° 2′, 78 m. SSE of Bombay. There are hot springs in the neighbourhood.

DATCHET, a parish in Bucks, 2 m. ESE of Windsor, on the E bank of the Thames, 24 m. from the Slough station of the Great Western railway, and on the line of the Windsor extension branch. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 922. There are several elegant villas in this p.
DATCHWORTH, a parish in Herts, 4 m. NE of Welwyn. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 581.

DATEV, a village and convent of Armenia, in the Russian prov. of Nakshivan, 20 m. W by S of Degh. The convent possesses 16 villages; and in 1830 derived a revenue from them of about 500 chetverts, = 150,000 lbs., of grain. The see of the bishop-superior of this convent is named, from the ancient prov. in which it is situated, Sunik.
DATNOVO, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 84 m. NW of Vilna, on an affluent of the Pevlieja.
DATSCHITZ, or DACZICZE, a village of Moravia, in the circle and 24 m. SSW of Iglau; on the r. bank of the Taya. Pop. 2,022. There is a fine chateau here belonging to the barons of Dalberg.
DATTENBERG, a village of Prussia, in the regency of Coblenz, circle of Neuwied. Pop. 320.

DATTOLI, a cluster of rocky islets to the E and SE of the island of Panaria, in the Lipari group. A little corn and flax is grown on the largest of the group called Basiluzzo. The islet Dattolo lies 14 m. off the E point of Panaria. These islets are supposed to be fragments of the periphery of an immense crater. Sicilian savans have found in them the long lost Evonymus of Plato.—Smyth.
DAUBA, or Duba, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Bunzlau, 16 m. NW of Jung-Bunzlau, at the foot of the Eichberg. Pop. 1,254.
DAUBRAWITZ, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 16 m. N of Brünn, on the l. bank of the Zwittawa. Pop. in 1834, 769.
DAUBRAWNIK, a town of Moravia, in the circle of Brünn, on the Schwazawa. Pop. in 1834, 750.

DAUDNAGAR, a district of the prov. of Bahar in Hindostan, on the r. bank of the Son. Area 327 sq. m. Pop. 160,600. It is poorly cultivated.—The cap., of the same name, is a large but poor place, situated on the Son, about 60 m. above its confluence with the Ganges. It contains a cloth factory.
DAUENDORF, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Haguenau. Pop. 1,221.
DAUGELICHKI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Vilna, district and 15 m. WSW of Vidzy.
DAUGI, or DAGHI, a village of Russia, in the gov. and 40 m. SSW of Vilna.
DAULAKEE. See DAHLIKI.

DAULE, a river of Ecuador, in the dep. of Guayaquil, which rises near San Miguel, and after a course from N to S of about 130 m., flows into the river of Guayaquil, 14 m. below the town of D. on its l. bank. It flows through a fertile valley. The environs of the town supply a large quantity of fruit for the Guayaquil markets.
DAULET-ABAD, a well-built fort of Persia, in the prov. of Khorasan, 12 m. SW of Damaghan.
DAUMA. See DAMMA.
DAUMAZAN, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Le Mas-d'Azil, on the 1. bank of the Ariège. Pop. 1,367.
DAUME', a small fortified island on the coast of | France, in the cant. and 2 m. S of Marseilles.
DAUMERAY, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Durtal. Pop. 1,853.
DAUN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the gov. and 29 m. NNE of Treves, near the r. bank of the Lieser. Pop. 500. Copper is mined, and there are mineral springs, in the vicinity.
DAUNTSEY, a parish in Wilts, 41 m. SSE of Malmsbury. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 576.
DAUPHIN, a village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, cant. and 4 m. S of Forcalquier. Pop. 600.

DAUPHIN, a county in the SE part of the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., bounded by the Susquehanna on the W and SW. Area 608 sq. m. Pop. 30,118. The cap. is Harrisburg.—Also a v. on the Susquehanna, 8 m. from Harrisburg.
DAUPHIN, a river of N. America, flowing into St. Martin's bay on the W side of Lake Winnipeg, in N lat. 52° 15', after passing through Lake Manitouba.
DAUPHIN (FORT), a settlement on the E coast of Madagascar, in S lat. 25° 1′, founded by the French, but now deserted.

DAUPHINE', an ancient prov. in the SE of France, now parcelled out amongst the deps. of Hautes-Alpes, L'Isère, and La Drome. It was di- 即田田田田山 vided into Haute-D., of which the cap. was Grenoble; and Bas-D., of which the cap. was Vienne. This prov. was ceded in 1343 by Humbert II. to Philippe, son of Philippe-de-Valois. Since the time of Charles V. till the dethronement of the elder branch of the 事 Bourbons in 1830, the eldest son of the king of France bore the title of the Dauphin from the ancient rulers of this prov. ğ.ğ.

DAUPHINE' (ISLE), one of a line of narrow islands 1 running parallel with the coast of Louisiana, along 13 the N shore of the gulf of Mexico. It is very flat, MI and composed only of a fine white sand; but fresh water can be had upon it in abundance. An early B French colony settled upon this island.

DAURI, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwah, on the N bank of the Nerbudda, in N lat. 22° 12'. The navigation of the river is here impeded by a fall of 30 ft. and the sudden contraction of the stream from a 1/2 m. to from 20 to 40 yds., in breadth.

DAURIKAN, a village of Asiatic Turkey, on the upland of Yeralah Göz, in the sanjak of Kastamuni, at an alt. of 3,240 ft. above sea-level. The Daurikan- Irmak rises near this v. and flows N to the Kizil- Irmak, through a fertile well-cultivated valley.
DAUSENAU, a village of Nassau, on the Lahn, 12 m. SE of Coblenz.
DAUSS. See Dass.
DAVANAGIRI, a small town and fort of Hindostan, in the Mysore territory, 36 m. NW of Chettledrug.
DAVENHAM, a parish in Cheshire, 2 m. S of Northwich, on the W bank of the Wenbeck. Area 9,440 acres. Pop. 5,335.
DAVENPORT, a township in the p. of Astbury in Cheshire, 4½ m. NW of Congleton. Area 660 acres. Pop. 125.

DAVENPORT, a township in Delaware co., in the state of New York, U. S., 69 m. SW of Albany. Pop. 2,052.—Also a v. in Scott co. in Iowa, on the N side of the Mississippi, 30 m. above Burlington. Immediately opposite D. is the town of Rock Island on the Illinois side; and above the two towns is the island itself. The river is here little more than a 1 m. in breadth.

DAVENPORT (Point), a low point forming the W extremity of Sturt bay, on the S coast of York's | thedral consists of 6 canons, who take equal shares DAV peninsula, in S. Australia. It is composed of a series of low sand-hills clothed with grass and oak trees, and about a 1 m. inland are salt-water lagunes. The bay is open to the E and SE, but is well sheltered by this point, and by the narrowness of the strait. It may be approached with safety; and presents good anchorage in 7 fath. on a sandy bottom, on its W side, at about 1 m. from the beach.

DAVENTRY, a parish and market-town in the hund. of Fawsley, co. of Northampton, 13 m. W by N of Northampton, and within 2 m. SW of the London and Birmingham railway, and the Grand Junction canal. Area of p. 4,090 acres. Pop. 4,565. The town occupies the ascent and summit of a hill, near the sources of the Avon and the Nen, which fall into the opposite seas. It is cleanly and respectable in appearance, with some good houses and shops. It is a polling-place for the S division of Northampton. The chief trade is shoemaking; and there is a considerable manufacture of whips. D. is considered the central horse-market in England.— Danes-hill, in the vicinity, is the most extensive Roman encampment in the kingdom.

DAVER-CONDAH, a town of Hindostan, cap. of a district of the same name, in the prov. of Hydrabad, near the l. bank of the Pedda Wag, 24 m. NW of the confluence of that river with the Kistna, and 55 m. SSE of Hydrabad. The district to which it gives its name is bounded on the S by the Kistna.

DAVEY (PORT), a large inlet on the SW coast of Van Diemen's Land. Its mouth is intersected by the parallel of 43° 19'S; and is 5 m. wide between the N and SE heads. It forms two arms; one, called Cockburn port, running N 8 m., with a breadth of 2 m.; and the other extending 10 m. E. Both these arms present good and well-sheltered anchorage; but the channel from which they diverge is fully exposed to the SW gales .- A river, called the Davey, flows into the head of Cockburn port from the NE.

DAVID RIVER, a river of Lower Canada, in the co. of Saguenay, which runs N, and falls into the Peribonea, 34 m. from the lake of St. John.—Also a river in the co. of Yamaska, formed by the union of the Ruisseau des Chênes and other small streams, and which discharges itself into the Yamaska a little above Isle Joseph.

DAVID'S (SAINT), a parish and city in the hund. of Dewisland, co. of Pembroke, 14 m. WNW of Haverfordwest, near the coast, to the N of St. Bride's bay. Besides the city or town of St. D., which consists in great measure of an assemblage of cottages intermingled with the ruins of ancient structures of a more imposing aspect, and some good modern streets, but situated in a wild and solitary district, the p. comprises the hamlets of Bychan, Gwylod-y- Wlad, Mawr, and Y-dee. Pop. in 1801, 1,803; in 1831, 2,388; in 1841, 2,413.—St. D. is an extensive diocese, comprehending the entire cos. of Pembroke, Carmarthen, Brecon, and—except 5 parishes—Radnor; together with some small parts of Monmouth, Hereford, Montgomery, and Glamorgan. It has 4 archdeaconries, viz., Brecon, Cardigan, Carmarthen, and St. D. The average gross yearly income of the see for 7 years, ending 31st Dec. 1835, was £3,616. The gross rev. in 1845 was £4,752. The number of benefices in this dio. in 1831, inclusive of sinecure rectories, but exclusive of benefices annexed to other preferments, was 416. The aggregate amount of the gross incomes of incumbents, in 409 of these benefices, was £60,653; average gross income £148. In 1840, 331 members of the clergy were non-resident. The number of curates in 1831 was 206: stipends, included in the income of incumbents, £11,464; average £55. The chapter or corporation of the caof the net revenue, which are paid in certain propor- | tions according to seniority. The average sum divided, after payment of the adoptions, is £1,176. The cathedral is a large Gothic structure of venerable appearance, 307 ft. in length, with a lofty square tower, surmounted by handsome pinnacles at each corner. The interior comprises a nave, and two side aisles, choir, and chancel. The nave is divided from the side aisles by a row of handsome columns, alternately round and octagon, five in number, with corresponding pilasters at each end, supporting 6 arches of ornamental Saxon architecture. Over these is a range of smaller Saxon pillars supporting other arches of less dimensions, reaching to the roof. The ceiling of the nave is of oak, divided into square compartments. Beyond the chancel, to the eastward, is the elegant chapel of Bishop Vaughan, built by that prelate in the time of Henry VIII., and exhibiting a fine specimen of the florid Gothic of that period. Adjoining this structure, and forming the extreme E end of the cathedral buildings, is St. Mary's chapel, now divested of its roof, and falling into rnins. The cathedral is enriched by a considerable number of ancient monuments. The cathedral precincts, though now ruinous, are extensive, and were once magnificent. A walled area, 1,200 yards in circumf., enclosed the episcopal palace, the cathedral, and the residences of various dignitaries. The present episcopal residence is at Abergwilly, in Carmarthenshire.
DAVID'S (SAINT), or LLANFAES, a parish in Breconshire, 8 m. W by S of Brecon. Pop. 1,422.
DAVID'S (SAINT), a small port of Fifeshire, in the p. of Dalgety, on the N coast of the frith of Forth, 2 m. E of Inverkeithing. A large quantity of coal is shipped here.
DAVID'S (SAINT), one of the Bermuda islands, in N lat. 32° 15′, W long. 64° 30′.

DAVID (St.), a parish of Jamaica, in the co. of Surrey. It is generally hilly, and is intersected in the N by the Grand ridge of the Blue mountains. The principal river by which it is watered is the Yullah, which runs SSE and falls into the sea at Cow-bay point. DAVID'S (St.) ISLANDS, or Free-will Is-
LANDS, a group of islands in the Asiatic archipelago, between New Guinea and the Pelew islands, in N lat. 0° 50′, E long. 134°.

DAVIDOV, a small town and fort of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 50 m. WNW of Viborg, district and 24 m. WSW of Vilmanstrand, in a low marshy locality, surrounded by lakes. The fort was built by Catherine II.
DAVIDOVO, a lake of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Yeniseisk, 80 m. E of Starokantaisk, and 200 m. NNE of Turukansk, to the SSE of and connected with the Piasina.

DAVIDSON, a central county of the state of Tennessee, U. S., comprising an area of 640 sq. m., intersected by Cumberland, Harpeth, and Stone rivers, and Whites and Poplar creeks. It has an undulating surface and a fertile soil. Pop. in 1830, 28,122; in 1840, 30,509. Its cap. is Nashville.—Also a township of Lycoming co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 109 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 370. - Also a township of Randolph co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 355.—Also a township of Lapeer co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 69.
DAVIDSON COLLEGE, a village of Mecklenburg co., in the state of North Carolina, U. S., 150 m. SW of Raleigh.
DAVIDSTOW, a parish of Cornwall, 4 m. NE of Camelford, N of the Inny. Area 6,260 acres. Pop. 408.
DAVIDSTOWN, a parish of co. Kildare, comprising an area of about 3,905 acres. Pop. 1,068. DAV

DAVIE, a central county of the state of North Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 175 sq. m., bounded on the E by Yadkin river, and drained by its branches. It has an undulating surface and a generally fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 7,574. Its cap. is Mocksville.

DAVIES, a county in the SW of the state of Indiana, U. S., between the E and W forks of White river, and watered by their branches. It has some fine prairies, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 6,720. Its cap. is Washington.—Also a county in the NW part of the state of Missouri, comprising an area of 576 sq. m., intersected by the W fork of Grand river. It is very fertile, and possesses a finely undulating surface. Pop. 2,736. Its cap. is Gallatin.
DAVILACAN, a town of the island of Luzon, Asiatic archipelago, on a bay of the E coast, 90 m. NNE of Manilla.
DAVINGTON, a parish of Kent, 3 of a mile NW of Faversham. Area 540 acres. Pop. 143.
DAVIOT, a parish of Aberdeenshire, 19 m. NW of Aberdeen, 31 m. in length, and about 2 m. in breadth. Pop. 643.
DAVIOT-AND-DUNLICHITY, a parish partly in the cos. of Inverness and Nairn, extending 23 m. in length on both sides of the Nairn, and varying from 2 to 4 m. in breadth. Pop. 1,681.
DAVIS, a small island of the Philippine group, to the SW of that of Bohol, in N lat. 9° 44'. Pearl oysters are found on its coasts.

DAVIS, a township of Lafayette co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. 673.—Also a township of Saline co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 312.— Also a township of Fountain co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 714.
DAVIS COVE, a river of Jamaica, in the p. of Hanover, co. of Cornwall, which runs WNW, and falls into a bay of the same name, 3 m. NE of Green harbour.

DAVIS' STRAIT, the name usually given to that portion of an extensive inlet of the Atlantic which lies between the parallels of 60° and 70° N, separates the W coast of Greenland from the E coast of Cumberland, and, running N by W, conducts into Baffin's bay on the N. Its entrance between Cape Farewell on the E, in N lat. 59° 45', W long. 47° 50' [Upton], and Cape Chudleigh on the W, in N lat. 60° 37', W long. 65°, is about 500 m. wide. Its narrowest part occurs nearly on the parallel of the Polar circle, where it is about 150 m. in width. The Greenland coast is everywhere bold and rocky; and is indented with numerous bights, creeks, and fiords or friths. Numerous islands skirt this coast of the strait, of which the most considerable is Disco, which is so high as to be visible on the horizon at the distance of 80 m. The quantity of floating ice on the W shore of the strait is of such extent as to present an almost insurmountable obstacle towards approaching the land in that direction to the N of Cape Walsingham. This ice, which has from time immemorial occupied nearly the same situation, is termed 'the West Pack,' and its southern portion 'the South-west ice.' The W coast is lower than the E, but rises gradually in the background into mountains of considerable height. This coast is still more irregular in outline than the Greenland side of the strait; and presents three great inlets to the S of Cape Walsingham, viz., Northumberland inlet, Frobisher's strait, and Hudson's strait. It is said that of late years the whale-fishery in Davis' strait has been most successfully pursued on the W side; and that the country in the interior is well-stocked with deer. It would appear, however, that the supply of fish in D. strait is rapidly lessening.— The current which runs along the E coast of Greenland, turning round Cape Farewell, trends N along the E coast of this strait, to about the parallel of Cape Walsingham, where it crosses to the W side, and then turns S along the W coast and the Labrader coast. Both shores of the strait are inhabited by Esquimaux tribes. "The winding and generally elevated coast of this strait has always produced in it the greatest varieties of ice, amongst which those of bergs and floes have been most conspicuous. The land-ice, during the rigour of winter, remains fixed in an unbroken sheet, stretching many miles to the sea. Under the warmth of spring and early summer, numerous fragments are detached, which, through the action of tides and currents, are floated out into the ocean, and sometimes reach even low latitudes on the shores of America. The greater proportion, however, coming from the opposite coasts, meet in mid-channel, and unite into a compact and continuous barrier, through which, till a very advanced period of the season, it is impossible for the navigator to penetrate. Between this central body, called the middle ice, and that シンタン シェンタング・ストライン ストイン・メイト・メイト・メイト・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・ attached to the land, there intervenes a narrow and precarious passage, always more or less encumbered, and often entirely closed up; yet through which the adventurous mariner can generally, in the course of two or three months, wind his course into the higher latitudes in the direction of Lancaster sound. Prior to the voyage of Ross and Parry, the range of operations had been limited to the SW fishery, and to that on the eastern shore as far as Horn sound. These fields, however, gradually shared the fate of those in the Greenland sea,—being, as it were, fished out; the whales having learnt to take refuge from the destroying power of their assailants in some more distant recesses of the Arctic ocean. When it was reported, therefore, by the officers employed in discovery, that, on the NW shores of Baffin's bay, fish had been seen in vast numbers, the spirit of enterprise was kindled, and success soon crowned its efforts. Since that period, not only the Greenland seas have been nearly deserted, but the original fishing-grounds in Labrador and Davis' strait have been regarded only as secondary objects; while every nerve has been strained to reach those interior shores, where whales are still found in an abundance elsewhere unexampled." [Polar Seas and Regions, p. 448.]—The strait takes its name from Davis, who, in 1587, ascended it to the parallel of 72° 16', where he found the variation of the compass to be nearly the same which it is at present, viz., 82° W. In 1616, Baffin passed through the strait, and advancing as high as the parallel of 78°, conferred his name on the great bay to the N of the strait.

DAVIS'S INLET, an indentation of the E coast of Labrador, to the N of Hopedale, in N lat. 55° 35'. It is 57 m. in depth, and 6 m. in medium breadth. On the NW side of this bay is a Moravian missionary establishment.
DAVISBOROUGH, a village of Washington co., in the state of Georgia, U.S., 41 m. SE of Milledgeville, on a central railroad.

DAVOIDGORODOK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 148 m. SSW of Minsk, district and 48 m. E of Pinsk, partly on an island formed by two branches of the Gorin, and partly on the l. bank of the E branch of that river. Pop. 3,000.

DAVOLI, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 20 m. SSW of Catanzaro. Pop. 2,775. It contains 3 churches, and has an annual fair. Cotton and silk are cultivated in its environs.

DAVOS, or Tafaas, a parish and village of Switzerland, cap. of the league of the Dix-Droitures, in the cant. of the Grisons, 15 m. ESE of Coire, in the valley of the same name, and at an alt. of 4,845 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 555. The triennial assembly of the landsgemeinde takes place here. The high jurisdiction of D. consists of the valley of the same name, running NE; and the four lateral valleys, of Fluelen, Disma, Sertig, and Monstein, comprising an area 21 m. in length, and 15 m. in its greatest breadth, from the Alps of the Grisons on the E, to the mountains of Schalfik on the W. The climate is extremely rigorous; snow to the depth of from 4 to 12 ft. covers the ground from April to Nov., and a fall of snow is not unusual even in summer. The D., or Landwasser, the principal river which waters the district, issues by a narrow defile from the SW extremity of the main valley, and unites with the Albula near Filisur. The rearing of cattle forms the chief object of rural industry. This jurisdiction contains 6 communes and 14 quartiers, and 1,805 inhabitants. The valley of D. was discovered and colonized in 1233 by some Valaisan hunters, and the German dialect of the Haut-Valais is still commonly spoken in the locality.
DAVOUIDOVA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Novgorod, district and 26 m. WSW of Teherepovetz.
DAVREL, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 71 m. N of Vilna, district and 45 m. NE of Vilkomir, in a marshy locality.
DAWDON, a township in the p. of Dalton-le- Dale, co. of Durham. Area 830 acres. Pop. 2,017.
DAWLE, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 14 m. ESE of Beraun, and 14 m. S of Prague, on the l. bank of the Moldau.

SW of Shiffnall, intersected by a branch of the Shrewsbury canal. Area 2,990 acres. Pop. 8,641. Pop. of township 4,485.
DAWLEY-PARVA, a township in the p. of Dawley-Magna, Salop. Pop. 1,435.

DAWN, a township of Upper Canada, in the Western District, intersected in the S by Bear creek, which is navigable to Dresden. The township abounds with white oak, maple, black walnut, and other varieties of the hardwood, and possesses a generally fertile soil. On the E branch of Bear creek is the settlement of Dawn or Taylor's mills, consisting in 1844 of about 10 houses.
DAWSON'S BRIDGE, a settlement of Upper Canada, near the centre of Dumfries co., Gore district, 6 m. from Galt. Pop. in 1844, 40.
DAWSON'S LAKE, a lake of Lower Canada in the Gore of Chatham, Ottawa co.
DAWULGAOM, a walled town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Berar, 65 m. NE of Aurungabad, at the entrance of a pass through the Berar mountains, and on the r. bank of the Pain Gunga.

DAX, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Landes.-The arrond. comprises an area of 231,864 hect., and contains 8 cant., viz. Castets, Dax, Saint-Esprit, Montfort, Peyrehorade, Pouillon, Souston, and Saint-Vincentde-Tyrosse. Pop. in 1831, 99,463; in 1846, 108,415. The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 17,847; in 1841, 20,051.—The town is pleasantly situated on the l. bank of the Adour, 33 m. SW of Mont-de- Marsan, and 28 m. NE of Bayonne. It is defended by old walls flanked with towers and a fortress. Of the buildings worthy of note, the principal are the cathedral, the hospital, and the bridge over the Adour, an edifice remarkable for the boldness of its structure. It has also a college, a theological seminary, a normal school, a museum, a theatre, &c., and has from the earliest period been noted for its thermal baths. The streets are straight and narrow, and the houses are chiefly of the old style of architecture. Pop. in 1792, 3,500; in 1841, 5,842. There are celebrated hot springs here of a temp. of 160°.
DAXABON, or DAJABON, a town of the island of St. Domingo, in the dep. of Cibao, on the r. bank of the Massacre, 39 m. ESE of Cape Hayti or St. Francis. Pop. 4,000.
DAXLANDEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle-Rhine, bail. and 4 m. W of Carlsruhe, on the Federbach, at an alt. of 1,210 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 1,488, of whom 1,439 are Catholics.
DAXWEILER, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency and 30 m. S of Coblenz, circle and 7 m. NNW of Kreuxnach. Pop. 400. It has an iron-work and a manufactory of sal-ammoniac.
DAY, a township of Saratoga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 63 m. NW of Albany, intersected by the Kayaderosseras mountains, and drained by the Sacandaga river. Pop. in 1840, 942.
DAY (St.). See GWENNAP.
DAYA, or Siddo, a town and port of the island of Sumatra, on the NW coast, to the S of Acheen, on a river of the same name.
DAYANVILLE, a village of Watson township, Lewis co., in the state of New York, U. S., 141 m. NW of Albany. Pop. in 1840, 125.
DAYLESFORD, a parish of Worcestershire, 31 m. SE of Stow-on-the-Wold. Area 540 acres. Pop. 81.
DAYMALOS, or Daimalos, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 19 m. ENE of Malaga, partido and 5 m. N of Velez-Malaga.

DAYTON, a township of Cattaraugus co., in the state of New York, U.S., 312 m. SW of Albany. It has an undulating surface, and is drained by Connewango creek and its tributaries. Its soil consists of 3 G clay and gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 946.—Also a township, cap. of Montgomery co., in the state of Ohio, 68 m. W by S of Columbus, watered by Great Miami river and its tributaries, the SW branch of Mad river, and Wolf creek. Pop. of township in 1840, 10,335. The village is situated on an island formed by Miami and Mad rivers, and is extremely flourishing. It possesses large cotton factories, paper and flour mills, distilleries, and other mills and manufactories, and is connected with Cincinnati by the Miami canal. Pop. in 1810, 383; in 1840, 6,067.— Also a village of Tippecanoe co., in the state of Indiana, 63 m. NW of Indianapolis. Pop. in 1840, 50.
DAYWELL, a township in the p. of Whittington, Salop. Pop. 328.

DEAD, a river of the state of Maine, U.S., which rises in Oxford co., near the boundary of Lower Canada, waters the SW part of Somerset co., and after a course of 70 m. falls into the Kennebec, 20 m. below Morsehead lake.—Also a stream of Penobscot co., in the same state, which falls into the Penobscot at Orono.

DEAD SEA (THE), or LAKE ASPHALTITES, the Sea of the Plain, and the Eastern sea of Scripture, the Bahr Lut or Birket Lut of the Arabs, a remarkable lake in Syria, between the parallels of 31° and 32° N, in the singular depression called El Ghor, which stretches far beyond it both on the N and S, forming, in fact, a deep and clearly defined valley, from 4 to 8 m. in width, from the N side of the lake of Tiberias to the Arabian gulf at Akabah. On the N, the D. sea is bounded by the Great plain; on the E by the mountains of Ammon and of Moab; on the S by the deserts of Idumea; and on the W by the mountains or desert of Judah. Its dimensions have been variously given, by both ancients and moderns. According to Mr. Bankes, its utmost length is not above 30 m. in a straight line from N to S; whilst Josephus and Diodorus Siculus make it double that length; and Pliny estimates it at 100 m. in length, and 25 m. in breadth. Dr. Robinson estimates it at 50 m. In the large map accompanying Captain Lynch's narrative—which is followed in the subjoined cut—the length of the sea measured from the head of the bight on the W side of the embouchure of the Jordan to the centre of its S extremity, where it receives a small stream from the S (u), being measured along a line running nearly N and S, but inclining to the W, is 41 m.; and the greatest breadth, 9\frac{1}{4} m., occurs a little to the S of Ain-Jidy (b), at half-distance from either extremity. Throughout two-thirds of its extent, it has an average breadth of 8 m.—It is probable that, like many other lakes, the D. sea has decreased, and is still decreasing; but observations are still wanting to establish this hypothesis. It appears to vary not less than 2 or 3 m. in extent in different years, and even at different seasons of the year, according as the flats towards its S extremity are more or less flooded in the rainy season; but the addition of even a dozen miles to the length of this sea would submerge the valley of the Jordan to above Jericho. Its depth, it is asserted by some travellers, never varies; others represent it as varying as much as 8 or 10 ft. at different seasons of the year. It lies in a deep caldron, surrounded by lofty cliffs of naked limestone rock, "blank hills piled high over hills, pale, yellow, and naked," [Eothen,] and exposed to the unclouded beams of a burning sun. "Whoever," says Stephens, "has seen the D. sea, will ever after have its aspect impressed on his memory; it is, in truth, a gloomy and fearful spectacle." "Here," says another traveller, "Death wields a leaden sceptre: the eye perceives only the absence of life: the ear is cheered by no sound, even the waveless sea sleeps in mysterious silence." [Elliott.] There is pro- 834 CONTRACTOR OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF bably a considerable tinge of imagination in the highwrought sketches of the peculiarly gloomy character of the D. sea. Lord Nugent's impressions at least appear to have partaken little of this character. "The sun," he says, "was shining brightly through the whole day which we spent in view of it and on its shores. Whether this alone may account for it or not, the colour of the sea, and of the mountains on each side, was by no means dark, nor their general appearance gloomy, as has been so often described. On the contrary, the mountains were tinged with a variety of hues, and the sea sparkled brilliantly in the sunbeams, and was clear as crystal even to its level shores." [Lands Classical and Sacred.] The Rev. J. D. Paxton, an American traveller, says on this point: "Instead of that dark, gloomy, and turbid spread of water that I had imagined, it struck me as a very pleasant lake. It reminded me of the beautiful lake of Nice." This sea, however, is more sudden in its changes, and varied in its aspects, than perhaps any other sheet of water on the surface of the globe. The encircling precipices in general descend abruptly into the lake, which, on account both of their height, and the natural density of its waters, is seldom agitated by the winds; and its shores are utterly barren, and therefore seldom visited by any footstep save that of the wild Arab, and he holds it in superstitious dread; but where fountains or streams of fresh water occur in the surrounding mountain territory, the Arab is often found cultivating a patch of barley, or a bed of cucumbers. Some parts of the coast are claimed by particular tribes, but the greater part appears to be common to roam- 0 1 JERICHO AND THE MOUTH The Mountains of Moad in the Distance SEA DEAD THE . . ing bands of marauders. The waters of this sea have, however, in all ages been the subject of many mysterious and remarkable narratives. According to the testimony of all antiquity, and of most modern travellers, no living thing, -no trace in fact of animal or vegetable life, -exists in this sea: Ehrenberg has, however, found in its water infusoria of fresh or brackish water species,—a fact, by the way, proving that this lake never formed any part of the general ocean. Any dead fish, or recent shells found in it, have doubtless been transported into it from the Jordan. It has even been affirmed that no bird can fly over it and live; and that its waters are poisonous, and spread pestilential vapours around its shores. But Lamartine, Stephens, Robinson, Lynch, and all recent travellers, unite in disproving these statements. Lamartine says: "It is affirmed there are neither fish in its bosom, nor birds on its shores. I know not: I indeed saw no gulls, nor any of those pretty white birds, like sea-doves, which skim all the day over the waves of the sea of Syria, and follow the caiques on the Bosphorus; but a few hundred paces from the D. sea I fired at and shot some birds similar to the wild drakes which rise from the marshy shores of the Jordan. If the air of the sea were mortal to them, they would not come so near to brave its mephitic vapours." Stephens, the enterprising and intelligent American traveller, relates that he saw a flock of gulls floating quietly on the bosom of the lake; and Lynch repeatedly observed swallows and gulls skimming across its surface. Doubtless the burning heat of the climate makes itself severely felt here; and, in connection with the adjacent marshes, frequently gives rise to intermittent fevers. The nature of its waters, likewise, prevents the growth on its margin of rushes, reeds, and osiers; while the sterility of the whole region, and the want of fish, and of other food suited to their sustenance, sufficiently account for the rarity of aquatic fowls. Yet there are many circumstances connected with the D. sea full of interest; and the enterprising traveller, the devout pilgrim, and the speculative philosopher, alike find ample matter for reflection and inquiry on visiting its shores. "Its density is so great that the wind causes no ripple on its surface; but calm and motionless, it 'reflects the light and the firmament like a Venetian mirror:' it is a sea of molten lead, reflecting the high and barren mountains which bound its sides." The ancients believed its density to be such that even metals would not sink if placed on its surface. Upon this subject Stephens remarks: "From my own experience, I can almost corroborate the most extravagant accounts of the ancients. I know, in reference to my own specific gravity, that in the Atlantic, or the Mediterranean, I cannot float without some little movement of my hands; and even then my body is almost totally submerged; but here, when I threw myself upon my back, my body was half-out of water. I could have lain there, and read with perfect ease." Dr. Olin says that he found it bear him upon its surface without any effort to sustain himself; and that parties unable to swim easily floated upon it. Modern chemical research explains the reason of this buoyant property of the waters of the D. sea. Its specific gravity, according to Marcet, is 1.211, that of rainwaters being 1.000; and an analysis of 100 grains of the water yielded M. Gay-Lussac estimated its specific gravity, at a temp. of 62° 6 F., at 1.2283, and obtained a saline residuum of 26.24 parts in 100 parts. The specific gravity of the water of the D. sea, it thus appears, is greater than that of any other saline lake on the surface of the globe, containing one-fourth of its weight in salts. If desiccated at a perfect temp. of 130°, they would amount to 41 per cent. of the water. The water of its great affluent, the Jordan, on the contrary, is extremely dissimilar: being soft, sweet, and light, and containing only a 300th part of the solid matter contained in the water of the lake. The water of the lake of Tiberias, likewise, is "sweet, cool, and most refreshing to the taste." [Clarke.] To the taste, the water of the D. sea is, as already remarked, extremely salt and bitter. Elliott compares it to "sea-salt mixed with Epsom salts and quinine;" Madden to "a solution of nitre mixed with an infusion of quassia." From some of its ingredients it likewise derives a pungency which irritates the skin, producing a sensation resembling that of prickly heat, and acting on the eyes as pungently as smoke. [Elliott.] Yet when taken up in a glass, it appears perfectly clear. The great specific gravity of the water likewise accounts for its reputed immobility. Bitumen, or asphaltum—which gave to the D. sea its classical name, Lacus Asphaltites—is scarce on the N shore, but on the E and W is picked up in considerable quantities. There seems reason to believe that portions of the bed of the lake, or of its immediate substratum, consist of bitumen, which from time to time is thrown up into the water when detached by any cause. Or it may be produced by springs at the bottom of the sea, where it coagulates, and rises to the surface from time to time. Sulphur also occurs on the borders of the lake, in pieces as large as walnuts; and a species of black shining stone, which is made into rosaries and toys at Jerusalem. The first attempt in modern times to explore this lake was by Mr. Costigan, an enterprising young Irishman. Attended by a Maltese sailor, Mr. C. sailed completely round it, taking the bearings of the coast, and crossed it repeatedly, sounding for its depth. He died soon after of an illness induced by fatigue and exposure on the lake; but the results of this voyage were communicated by the surviving companion of his labours, whom Mr. Stephens sought out at Beirut. The account given by Mr. Costigan's attendant was to the following effect:—Every day they sounded, frequently with a line 175 brachia, of about 6 ft. each; they found the bottom rocky and of very unequal depth, sometimes ranging 30, 40, 80, 20 brachia, all within a few boats' length; sometimes the lead brought up sand, like that of the mountains on each side; they failed in finding bottom but once, and in that place there were large bubbles all around for 30 paces, rising probably from a spring; in one place they found, on the bank, a hot sulphur spring; at the S extremity Mr. C. looked for the river of Dogs, but did not find it; in four different places they found ruins, and could clearly distinguish large hewn stones, which seemed to have been used for buildings. Dr. Robinson visited the D. sea on two occasions, carefully examining the W shore from Ain Jidy [Engedi] to Jericho, and the entrance of the Jordan; on the second visit, visiting the S end in his journey down the Ghor and the Arabah to Wadi-Musa. In 1838, a Mr. Moore and his party obtained permission from the pasha of Egypt to transport a boat from the Mediterranean to the Dead sea, which they effected, and embarking on the 29th of March, explored the sea in shores. It is to be regretted that the results of this exploration have as yet been but partially communicated to the public. In the spring of the year 1848, Capt. Lynch, of the United States brig Supply, obtained permission from the Ottoman government to explore Lake Tiberias, the river Jordan, and the D. sea. The recommendations given by the sultan to the different officers of the places visited, obtained for his party every assistance required; and the results of this expedition have been given to the public in a pretty large volume. The expedition, composed of 15 persons, landed at St. Jean d'Acre, from which place they proceeded to the sea of Galilee, or Lake Tiberias, distant some 40 m. They had with them two metal boats,—one of copper, the other of iron. On the road they had to climb several mountains, and cross formidable ravines; on more than one occasion they were forced to lower the boats by means of ropes down frightful precipices; but all their difficulties were surmounted by the patience and address which distinguish the true sailor; and on the 8th of April the two boats floated on the deep blue waves of the sea of Galilee. They found the navigation of the Jordan very difficult and dangerous, on account of its fearfully rapid currents. Some idea of the fall of the Jordan between Lake Tiberias and the D. sea may be conceived from the fact, that its bed, in a direct distance of 60 m., winds nearly 200 m; while the difference in the level of the two seas, or lakes, is upwards of 1,000 ft. The expedition, however, accomplished the descent of the Jordan in 8 days. The Jordan enters the D. sea almost close to the E ridge of mountains. Its banks as it approaches the lake are strongly impregnated with salt and bitumen. [Wilson.] For a few hundred yards from its mouth (J) the water of the Jordan is sweet; while the waters of the D. sea are without smell, but to the taste are nearly as bitter, salt, and disgusting, as at any part further down. A small low point of land, at the mouth of the Jordan (a), on the r. bank, formed probably by the deposits, projects a little way into the sea, forming two bays, of which the W is the most extensive. Of course the volume of water with which the Jordan enters the lake varies greatly with the period of the year at which it is observed. Dr. Shaw, estimating its average breadth at 30 yards, its depth at 9 ft., and its current at 2 m. an hour, calculated that it discharged into the D. sea daily 6,090,000 tuns. Dr. Halley calculated that the absorption from the surface of the Mediterranean was equal to 6,914 tuns daily per sq. in.; and unquestionably the evaporation from the surface of the D. sea must be conducted at a still more rapid rate than from that of the Mediterranean. The N shore of the lake is an extensive mud-flat with a sandy plain beyond, and is covered with branches and trunks of trees, some charred and blackened as by fire, others incrusted with a coating of salt. As seen from the shore, at its head, the D. sea is a beautiful sheet of water, with a breadth of about 6 m., stretching off to the S between towering and frowning bulwarks that bound the view on the N and E, and seem to approach each other so as to form the S limit of the sea at the distance of 12 or 15 m. Dr. Robinson thus describes the scenery of the lake as seen from above Ain Jidy (b), a point about half-way down the W shore: "Turning aside a few steps to what seemed a small knoll upon our r., we found ourselves on the summit of a perpendicular cliff overhanging Ain Jidy [Engedi] and the sea, at least 1,500 ft. above its waters. The D. sea lay before us in its vast deep chasm, shut in on both sides by ranges of precipitous mountains; their bases sometimes jutting out into the water, and again retreating so as to leave a narrow strip of shore below. The view included the whole S half of the sea, quite to its extremity; and also, as we afterwards found, the greater portion of the N half; although the still higher projecting cliff of El Mersed (c) intervened on 836 our l. to prevent our seeing the extremity of the sea in that direction. One feature of the sea struck us immediately, which was unexpected to us, viz. the number of shoal-like points and peninsulas which run out into its S part, appearing at first sight like flat sand-banks or islands. Below us, on the S, were two such projecting banks on the W shore, composed probably of pebbles and gravel, extending out into the sea for a considerable distance. The larger and more important of these is on the S of the spot called Birket-el-Khulîl (d), a little bay or indentation in the W precipice, where the water, flowing into shallow basins when it is high, evaporates, and deposits salt. This spot is just S of the mouth of Wady-el-Khubarah. Opposite to this, nearly in the middle of the sea, is a long low narrow bank (C to M), also apparently composed of pebbles and gravel, running from NE to SW, and joined towards the S end to the E shore by an isthmus of some breadth. This long peninsula extends towards the S beyond the W shoal or point above described; so that from the spot where we now stood, they seemed to interlock, and we saw the end of the peninsula across the point of the shoal. Toward the S extremity of the sea, a long low mountain was seen running out obliquely towards the SSE, extending from near the W cliffs apparently to the middle of the Ghor (u). This our Arabs called Hajr Usdum,— 'Stone of Sodom,'-and said it was composed wholly of rock salt, too bitter to be fit for cooking, and only used sometimes as a medicine for sheep." "The features now described," Dr. R. resumes, "together with the flat shores, give to the whole S part of the sea the appearance, not of a broad sheet of water, but rather of a long winding bay, or the estuary of a large river, when the tide is out and the shoals left dry. Only a comparatively narrow channel remained covered with water. This channel of the sea (so to speak) is in some parts quite narrow, and winds very much. Between the points of the W shoal and the peninsula, the distance cannot certainly be more than one-fourth or perhaps one-sixth of the whole breadth of the sea, if so much. The direction of the peninsula, and then that of Usdum, causes the channel apparently to sweep round first towards the W and afterwards towards the E, giving to this portion of the sea a very irregular form. Our Arabs, -both the Ta'âmirah and Rashâideh,-knew of no place where the sea could be forded. As we looked down upon it from this lofty spot, its waters appeared decidedly green, as if stagnant, though we afterwards saw nothing of this appearance from below. A slight ripple was upon its bosom, and a line of froth was seen along and near the shore, which looked like a crust of salt." [Biblical Researches.] The S part of the D. sea has a remarkable configuration. The long and singular peninsula noticed in the above extract from Dr. Robinson's work presents itself here. It is connected with the E shore by a low neck. The bay extending further S is in many parts very shallow, seldom exceeding 2 fath.; while beyond there are low flat soft shores over which the lake, when swollen by the rains of winter, sets for several miles. The whole of this part of the sea is like the winding estuary of a large river when the tide is out and the shores are dry [Robinson]. At this end of the sea, the remarkable ridge called Usdum (u u) or Asdum, -a word in which the name of Sodom occurs anagrammatically [Dr. John Wilson], -presents a solid mass of rock salt in one uneven rugged ridge varying from 100 to 150 ft. in height, and about 5 geog. m. in length. The existence of such an immense quantity of this mineral on the shores of a sea comparatively inconsiderable in magnitude accounts for the excessive saltness of its waters. Captain Lynch thus describes the view from the anchorage off Usdum: "It was indeed a scene of unmitigated desolation. On one side, rugged and worn, was the salt mountain of Usdum, with its conspicuous pillar (P), which reminded us at least of the catastrophe of the plain; on the other, were the lofty and barren cliffs of Moab, in one of the caves of which the fugitive Lot found shelter. To the S was an extensive flat intersected by sluggish drains, with the high hills of Edom semi-girding the salt plain where the Israelites repeatedly overthrew their enemies; and to the N was the calm and motionless sea, curtained with a purple mist, while, many fath. deep in the slimy mud beneath it, lay embedded the ill-fated ruins of Sodom and Gomorrah. The glare of light was blinding to the eye, and the atmosphere difficult of respiration. No bird fanned with its wing the attenuated air, through which the sun poured his scorching rays upon the mysterious element on which we floated, and which alone of all the works of its Maker contains no living thing within it. While in full view of the peninsula, I named its N extremity Point Costigan (C), and its S one Point Molyneaux (M), as a tribute to the memories of the two gallant Englishmen who lost their lives in attempting to explore this sea." At one-third the distance from the N extremity of Usdum, Capt. Lynch's party observed a lofty round pillar, standing at the head of a deep narrow abrupt chasm. On examination it proved to be of solid salt capped with carbonate of lime, cylindrical in front, and pyramidal behind. The upper or rounded part was about 40 ft. high, and rested on a kind of oval pedestal from 40 to 60 ft. above the level of the sea. A prop or buttress connected it with the mountain behind.—The American expedition made the tour of the D. sea and returned to the point of its departure, with the boats in the same condition as when they left New York, and all the crew in good health. The following are the principal facts elicited by this expedition: The bottom of the N part of the D. sea is almost a plain. The meridional lines, at a short distance from the shore, vary little in depth; the greatest depth found was 195 fath. Mr. Moore's party reported that they had found a depth of 400 fath. Near the shore, the bottom is generally a saline incrustation; but the intermediate portion is of soft mud, mixed with rectangular crystals, most frequently cubes, of pure salt. On one occasion they obtained only crystals with the lead line. - In the same proportion that the N part of the D. sea is deep, the S part is shallow, to the extent that for a quarter of its length the depth was found to be but 18 ft. Its S part presented no crystallizations, but its shores are covered with incrustations of salt, and, on landing, the footmarks in an hour's time were covered with crystallizations. —The shores in face of the peninsula, and its W side, present evident marks of destruction.—Another fact not less curious is, that the bottom of the D. sea forms two sunken plains,—one elevated, the other depressed.—There are no islands in this sea, although Stephens, Warburton, and Wilson all assert that they saw one from the head of the lake. There are however some peninsular projections connected with the mainland by low narrow isthmuses, which, when the connecting link is overflowed, must present the appearance of islands. After the examination of the D. sea, the American expedition proceeded to determine the height of the mountains, and the level of a plain, from Jerusalem to the Mediterranean sea. The fact of the depression of the D. sea below the Mediterranean appears to have been first noticed by Messrs. Moore, whose experiments in March 1837, by means of the boiling point of water, indicated a depression of about 500 ft. [Geog. Journ. 1837 and 1839]. In the month of April, in the same year, Schubert's observations with the barom. gave a depression of about 600 ft. Lieut. Symonds of the British engineers had previously carried a line of levels across from Jaffa to the D. sea by two different routes; and the results, corresponding to within an insignificant fraction, gave 1,312.2 ft. for the depression of the D. sea below the level of the Mediterranean, while the lake of Tiberias he found was only 328 ft. below the Mediterranean. M. Collier, from the observations of M. Bertou, had likewise calculated the depression of the D. sea below the Mediterranean at 1,249.8 Parisian, or 1,330 English ft. The results of Capt. Lynch's admeasurements with the spirit-level, from the chasm of the D. sea through the desert of Judea were confirmatory of Lieut. Symonds' triangulation, making the difference of level, or the depression of the surface of the D. sea below that of the Mediterranean, to be a little over 1,300 ft. Destruction of the cities of the Plain.] Since the days of Burckhardt it has been supposed that there was no lake in the valley of the Jordan previous to the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah; and that the river Jordan flowed through El-Arabah, the great valley of Edom, to the E branch of the Red sea; but this theory can be no longer regarded as tenable. The level of the Dead sea being now ascertained to be vastly below that of El- Arabah and the Red sea, it is impossible in the nature of things that the Jordan could ever have flowed through it, and a lake consequently must have existed in the plain ever since the creation. "We were exactly," says Dr. Wilson, "seven hours in crossing the Arabah. Cutting it diagonally, we did not find it so level on its surface as we expected; and, generally speaking, it is as barren as the desert itself. It has commonly a very hard stony bottom. Patches of softer material, but of sand with very little soil in it, here and there occur, especially where there are depressions on its surface. Many boulders and rounded stones of red and white granite, porphyry, basalt, sandstone, and lime, such as are found in beds of rivers running between mountains of different formations, are in many parts scattered over its surface. On the E side there are beds of alluvial gravel torn by the torrents. The dry bed of one of these torrents, with steep banks, called Wadf-el-Gharandel, we found about 1 m. in breadth. That this was the bed of a river we had no doubt; and we were quite willing to believe that it must be the bed of the ancient Jordan, through which its rolling floods passed on to the Red sea before the destruction of the cities of the plain. The very name which it bears, however, when viewed in connexion with its real source, puts an end to this interesting speculation. It is called the Wadí Gharandel (Arindela) because it is the continuation of a large wadi and winter-torrent coming down first in a NW direction from the heights of Mount Seir, and then, on arriving at the level of the Arabah, not passing to the SW to the Red sea, but to the NE to the Dead sea. It is considerably to the S of the part where it enters the great plain that the drainage of the Arabah goes to the Red sea. Some may think that these facts, whatever they may determine as to the Wadi Gharandel as it now exists, do not conclusively prove that the Jordan may not have passed through the Arabah to the Red sea before the overwhelming of Sodom, Gomorrah, and the other cities of the plain. On the occasion of this catastrophe, they may say, a great alteration may have taken place in the level of the valley throughout its whole extent. An elevation may have occurred in its middle, and a depression in its N parts. The extent of this elevation and depression necessary to suit the facts of the case, it is to be observed in reply, is such as far to transcend the Scripture narrative. The D. sea has been found by the actual measurements of Lieut. Symonds of the Royal Engineers, to be 1.312.2 ft., and the lake of Tiberias 328.98 ft., below the level of the Mediterranean. If all this depression took place with the raising of the Wadi Arabah above the level of the ocean, when the Lord rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire out of heaven, then must God have not only 'overthrown those cities and all the plain (in which they were), and all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew upon the ground,' as the sure word of his testimony informs us; but, if we may judge from anything we know of the mighty power of an earthquake of the required magnitude, it must, if it took place, have convulsed to their overthrow the whole lands of Canaan, Moab, Ammon, Edom, and the Desert, to the destruction of all their inhabitants. No such convulsion took place. Lot, casting his eyes on Zoar, quite proximate to Sodom, said, 'This city is near to flee unto, and it is a little one: oh, let me escape thither (is it not a little one?), and my soul shall live.' Into this city he was permitted to flee, and was safe. Abraham, living in the plains of Mamre, near Hebron, had practical cognizance of the execution of the threatened vengeance of God on the cities of the plain, only by his getting up early in the morn ing to the place where he stood before the Lord, and looking towards Sodom and Gomorrah, and beholding the smoke of the country going up like the smoke of a furnace. Striking as must have been the phenomena which occurred during the storms of fire and brimstone, and the eruptions and submergence which may have been their cause or accompaniments, they certainly fall short of the awful demands of the theory to which I refer. The fact undoubtedly is, that the Wadi 'Arabah and its continuation, the valley of the Jordan, whatever partial changes they may have undergone in our own Adamic era, together form perhaps the most wonderful crevasse in the whole world—a fissure made by volcanic and basaltic eruptions, long before the race of man appeared on the globe." [Lands of the Bible.] The D. sea consists, as we have seen, of two lakes, an upper and a lower, the division betwixt them being strongly marked by a contraction in the mountains which run along the shore on both sides, and a rising in the bottom. Mr. Wylie supposes that, previous to the destruction of Sodom, the lake terminated at what is now the dividing line, leaving between its S extremity and the head of the Arabah a fine fertile plain of about 20 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth—the scriptural valley of Siddim, where the cities stood, and where Lot fed his flocks. Sodom and her neighbour-cities stood, he supposes, at the head of the plain, near what was then the S termination of the lake; and the destruction falling on this part of the plain, the ground was thereby depressed, and the waters of the lake pouring in, covered the site of the guilty cities, which was thus permanently added to the lake, and forms what is now the lower lake. In support of this theory, Mr. Wylie adduces the evidence offered by the geographical structure of the region, which so plainly testifies that the D. sea is composed of two distinct lakes, that may have had their origin at different times, and from different causes. He shows, also, from the time that Lot took in passing between Sodom and Zoar on the morning on which the former was destroyed, that Sodom could not have been more than 6 m. distant from Zoar; and the site of Zoar being known at this day, on the S shore, Sodom and the other cities which were ranged round her, no doubt at short distances, must have stood within what are now the limits of the lower lake. Zoar, or Tzoar, is said by Eusebius to have been at the S end of the D. sea; and the description in the 10th ver. of Gen. xiii. shows that the country was well-watered to Zoar. This theory is also corroborated by the aspect of that portion of the plain which is yet traceable. It is watered by numerous streams, and it is easy to imagine that before the destruction, its aspect was beautiful in the extreme, such as to tempt Lot to choose it for the range of his flocks, and such as merited the commendation which Moses passed upon it, "well watered everywhere, even as the garden of the Lord;" or, as the passage might more clearly be rendered, "And Lot, raising his eyes, saw all the plain of the Jordan, which, before the Lord had destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, was watered everywhere, until we came to Tzoar, like the garden of the Lord, and like the land of Egypt." Of the manner of the destruction Mr. Wylie says, "Lightning might ignite the bituminous soil of the plain—and that such was the nature of the soil appears from the history in Genesis; and, if once ignited, the whole valley would speedily be converted into one vast blazing furnace, in which cities, men, and the ground itself, would perish. The burning would go on till the bituminous strata were destroyed, and thus a basin would be formed for the lower lake. This manner of destruction would cause the valley to present the appearance which it did to Abraham, when he viewed it in the morning from the heights of Hebron. 'He looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah, and toward all the land of the plain, and behold, and lo, the smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace.' A thick heavy smoke must have arisen from a burning bituminous mass of about 10 m. by 6. Had the ground been suddenly submerged, as some believe, the smoke would have been cut off. This also explains the means the Almighty was pleased to employ, to limit the destruction to the upper part of the plain where the cities stood, that there the wells of bitumen chiefly abounded which had mainly contributed to the formation of the bituminous soil." So many theories have been started as to the 'apples of Sodom.' that conjectures respecting this alleged vegetable production of this region may appear superfluous. Among the various fruits which have been so called is one called 'Asher by the Arabs, the Asclepias gigantea, which grows in great abundance in the vicinity of Elisha's fountain and Jericho, and in the valley of the Jordan. It is in flower, and has also ripe seed or fruit, all the year round. The seed is of a yellow colour, beautiful to the eye, but exceedingly unpalatable, and reputed to be poisonous; the appearance, however, of its interior does not quite correspond to the description of the fruit in question. The fruit of the Solanum melongena, or mad apple, is regarded by Hasselquist as the true apple of Sodom. When this is attacked by a certain insect, the skin is pierced with a hole scarcely perceptible, and remains apparently perfect and of a beautiful colour, while the inside is converted into a powder like dust. Mr. Curzon conceives that he has succeeded in discovering this long-sought-for fruit. "We made," he says, "a somewhat singular discovery when travelling among the mountains to the E of the D. sea, where the ruins of Ammon, Jerash, and Adjeloun well repay the labour and fatigue encountered in visiting them. It was a remarkably hot and sultry day; we were scrambling up the mountain through a thick jungle of bushes and low trees, when I saw before me a fine plum-tree, loaded with fresh blooming plums. I cried out to my fellow-traveller, 'Now, then, who will arrive first at the plum-tree?' and as he caught a glimpse of so refreshing an object we both pressed our horses into a gallop to see which would get the first plum from the branches. We both arrived at the same moment, and, each snatching at a fine ripe plum, put it at once into our mouths, when, on biting it, instead of the cool, delicious, juicy fruit which we expected, our DEA mouths were filled with a dry bitter dust, and we sat under the trees upon our horses sputtering and hemming, and doing all we could to be relieved of the nauseous taste of this strange fruit. We then perceived, and to my great delight, that we had discovered the famous apple of the D. sea, the existence of which has been doubted and canvassed since the days of Strabo and Pliny, who first described it. Many travellers have given descriptions of other vegetable productions which bear some analogy to the one described by Pliny; but up to this time no one had met with the thing itself, either upon the spot mentioned by the ancient authors or elsewhere. I brought several of them to England. They are a kind of gall-nut. I found others afterwards upon the plains of Troy; but there can be no doubt whatever that this is the apple of Sodom to which Strabo and Pliny referred. Some of those which I brought to England were given to the Linnæan society, who published an engraving of them and a description of their vegetable peculiarities in their Transactions; but, as they omitted to explain the peculiar interest attached to them in con sequence of their having been sought for unsuccessfully for above 1,500 years, they excited little attention; though, as the evidence of the truth of what has so long been considered as a vulgar fable, they are fairly to be classed among the most curious productions which have been brought from the Holy land." Authorities.]-Biblical Researches in Palestine, &c. By E. Robinson, D. D., vol. ii.—Travels in Austria, Russia, and Turkey. By C. B. Ethiott. Lond., 2 vols. 8vo., 1838.—Geog. Journal, vols. viii. and xviii. —Schubert, Reise in das Morgenland in 1836-7. Erlang., 1839.— Bullet, de la Soc. Geog., Jan. 1839, vol. x.—Travels in Egypt, &c. By the Rev. S. Olin, D. D. New York, 2 vols. 8vo., 1843.—Lands of the Bible. By John Wilson, D. D. Edin., 2 vols. 8vo, 1847 .- Narrative of the U.S. Expedition to the River Jordan and the Dead Sea. By W. F. Lynch, U. S. N. Philad., 1849, 8vo.

DEADMAN'S ISLE, an islet or rock in the gulf of St. Lawrence, in the group of the Magdalene islands, 73 m. from the main chain, and to the W of Amherst island, in N lat. 47° 10', and W long. 62°. In shape it is an irregular prism, about 300 fath. long by 1 wide, and rising 170 ft. above the sea.
DEADMAN'S POINT, a headland of Cornwall, 13 m. NE of Falmouth, in N lat. 50° 30'.

DEAKOVER, or DIAKOVAR, a town of Hungarian Slavonia, in the comitat of Verocze, 20 m. SSW of Esseg. Pop. 2,050. It is ill built, but contains a handsome eathedral and archiepiscopal palace. It has also an episcopal lyceum, and a theological seminary.

DEAL, a parish, borough, market-town, and seaport, in the hundreds of Cornilo and Bewsborough, co. of Kent, but having separate jurisdiction as a member of the Cinque-port of Sandwich. Area of p. 1,120 acres. Pop. in 1801, 5,420; in 1831, 7,268; in 1841, 6,688.—The town of D., strictly so called, 8 m. NNE of Dover, consists of Upper, Middle, and Lower D. The latter, containing the great mass of the pop., extends along the shore, and is connected with Upper D. by a row of houses called Middle D. The houses in Upper and Middle D. are detached, and are inhabited by the wealthier classes. There is here a naval store-house, and a custom-house. The gross receipt of customs for 1839 was £229 9s. 5d.; for 1846, £1,047. D. has no harbour, but the ample roadstead called the Downs, between it and the Goodwin sands, furnishes a most convenient anchorage for shipping. Inward and outward bound vessels generally touch at D., to land or take in provisions, passengers, letters, &c.; it has also long been famed as a station for pilots. Boat-building and sail-making form the chief occupation of the pop. By the parliamentary boundary act, the adjoining p. of Walmer was united to D., and these, together with the port and town of Sandwich, return 2 members to parliament.—This town has been considered by some to have been the place of Julius Cæsar's landing when he invaded the island, 55 B. C., but this is doubtful; Mennius says, "Cæsar fought at Dola," which Camden supposes to mean Deal; but there are no authentic records of a town existing here until centuries after the Romans quitted Britain. D. was annexed to the Cinque-ports at an early period after the Norman conquest.
DEAL, a watering village of Shrewsbury township, Monmouth co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., on Poplar Swamp creek, 1 m. from the sea.

DEAN, a parish and township of Cumberland, 5 m. SW of Cockermouth, bounded on the W by a branch of the Derwent. Area of p. 6,360 acres. Pop. 876. Pop. of township 226.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Spelsbury, Oxfordshire. Pop. 141.

DEAN, a river in the counties of Leicester and Nottingham, which falls into the Trent at Newark .-- Also a river of Forfarshire, which issues from the lake of Forfar; runs SW; receives the water of Gairie; and after a total course of 12 m., discharges itself into the Isla about a mile N of Meigle.—Also a suburb of Edinburgh.
DEAN-BOTTOM, a hamlet in the p. of Horton- Kirby, Kent. Pop. 63.

DEAN (EAST), a parish of Hants, 64 m. NW of Romsey, on the Salisbury and Southampton canal. Area 640 acres. Pop. 228.—Also a p. of Sussex, 6 m. SE of Midhurst. Area 4,670 acres. Pop. 433.— Also a p. of the same co., 3 m. SW of Eastbourne, N of Beachy-head. Area 2,510 acres. Pop. 360.

DEAN-FOREST, an extra-parochial liberty in the hund. of St. Briavell's, co. of Gloucester, 5 m. SW by W of Newnham, belonging to the Crown, and rich in iron, coal, and other minerals. It comprehends 22,000 acres of land, of which about one-half are under enclosure for navy-timber. Great quantities of oak and beech have long been supplied by this forest, which was once reckoned the chief support of the navy. With the exception of the orchards, which abound throughout the vale and forest, and which produce great quantities of excellent cider, the remainder of this extensive district consists of open eommon. It is divided into 6 extra-parochial districts, viz.: Denby-walk, Herbert's-walk, Little Deanwalk, Speech-House-walk, York-walk, and Worcester-walk. The boundaries, having varied at different periods, were settled by act 16° Charles I., c. 6; and finally recognised by 20° Charles II., e. 3.—The forest is intersected by several railways. The Forest of D. railway runs from the Severn, near Newnham, into the forest, where three short branches radiate forth to separate mines.—The Severn and Wye railway runs from Lidbrook - on - the - Wye, bisects the forest, and terminates at Lydney. It is connected with the Severn at Naas point by a canal 1 m. in length.—The Monmouth railway runs from Monmouth into the forest, where several branch-railways proceed through it in different directions. The uses of all these railways consist in the conveyance of timber, coals, iron-ore, and other minerals found in the forest, for shipment, &c. The railway companies pay rents to the Crown.
DEAN (LITTLE), a parish of Gloucestershire, 2 m. NNW of Newnham, on the verge of Dean-forest. Area 780 acres. Pop. 828.
DEAN (MITCHELL), a parish of Gloucestershire, Il m. W of Gloucester. Area 680 acres. Pop. 665.
DEAN-PRIOR, a parish of Devonshire, 4 m. SSW of Ashburton, S of the Dart. Area 3,760 acres. Pop. 552.
DEAN-PRIOR'S, a parish of Hants, 4 m. NW of Petersfield. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 163.
DEAN (UPPER and NETHER), a parish of Bedfordshire, 31 m. W of Kimbolton. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 541.
DEAN-WALK (LITTLE), an extra-parochial district of Dean-forest, Gloucestershire. Pop. 2,597.

DEAN (WEST), a parish, partly in Hants and Wilts, 7 m. NW of Romsey, on the Salisbury canal. Area 2,780 acres. Pop. 426.—Also a p. of Sussex, 6 m. S of Midhurst. Area 4,480 acres. Pop. 657.— Also a p. of the same co., 25 m. NE of Seaford, bounded on the W by the Cuckmere. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 129.
DEANBURNHAUGH, a village in the p. of Ro- DEB berton, partly in the counties of Selkirk and Roxburgh. Pop. 86.

DEANE, or Dean, a parish of Hants, 5 m. SW of Basingstoke, near the Southampton and London railway. Area 1,350 acres. Pop. 164.—Also a p. of Lancashire, 13 m. WSW of Great Bolton, on the Bolton and Leigh railway, and near the post-road from Manchester to Lancaster. Area 19,340 acres. Pop. 26,217.
DEANHAM, a township in the p. of Hartburn, Northumberland, 10 m. SW of Morpeth, and S of the Wansbeck. Pop. 43.
DEAN'S CANAL, an inlet on the W coast of British North America, communicating with Salmon river, in N lat. 52°.

DEAN'S ISLAND, an island, or rather islandchain composed of a number of small islands joined together by coral reefs, of which the E point is in S lat. 15° 16′ 30″, W long. 147° 12′; the W point in S lat. 15°, W long. 148° 22'. It seems to be the same with that called by Byron PRINCE OF WALES ISLAND, and is also named OANNA.
DEANSVILLE, a village of Marshall township, Oneida co., in the state of New York, U. S., 104 m. WNW of Albany, on the Chenango canal. Pop. in 1840, 200.

DEARBORN, a county of the state of Indiana, U. S., comprising an area of 380 sq. m., bordered on the E by the Ohio, and drained by Great Miami and Whitewater rivers, and several smaller streams. It is generally hilly, but on the banks of the rivers is extremely fertile. Pop. in 1830, 13,974; in 1840, 19,327. Its cap. is Lawrenceville.—Also a township of Kennebec co., in the state of Maine, 15 m. N of Augusta. A large pond, which contains a number of beautiful islands, covers a considerable proportion of its surface. Pop. in 1840, 168.—Also a township of Wayne co., in the state of Michigan, intersected by the Rouge river. Pop. 1,248.

DEARBORN (FORT), a military station in the state of Illinois, U.S., on the S bank and near to the mouth of Chicago river, in N lat. 41° 50', W long. 87° 55', at an alt. of 591 ft. above sea-level. The mean annual temp. here is about 46° 14'.
DEARBORNVILLE, a village of Dearborn township, Wayne co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., on the S branch of Rouge river, consisting in 1840 of about 60 dwellings, and containing the U.S. arsenal.
DEARHAM, a parish and township of Cumberland, 5 m. NW of Cockermouth, on the Maryport and Carlisle railway. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 1,803. Pop. of township 1,037.
DEARNE. See Yorkshire.
DEASE'S INLET, an indentation of the N coast of America, between Point Christie and Point Tangent, in N lat. 71° 5', W long. 155° 10'.

DEASE'S RIVER, a river of British North America, which forms the outlet of a lake of the same name, and falls into the NW branch of Liard's river. —Also a river which rises in the Copper mountains, and flows into the NW side of Great Bear lake, at the upper end of Dease bay. It is the principal feeding stream of the lake.

DEASE AND SIMPSON'S STRAIT, a channel of British North America, between Adelaide peninsula and the S coast of the mass of land which is supposed to be united to Boothia Felix by Boothia isthmus. Its W entrance is intersected by the parallel of 68° 33' N, and the meridian of 97° 50' W. Its E extremity lies between Booth point on the N, and Ogle point on the S.
DEAVERTOWN, a village of York township, Morgan co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 71 m. SE of Columbus. Pop. in 1848, 200.

DEBA, a river of Spain, which has its source in the Sierra Sejos, on the N confines of the prov. of | Palencia; runs N through the prov. of Santander; and falls into the bay of Biscay, at a small port of the same name. The town is prettily situated amidst wooded hills, and has a fine church.
DEBA, a town and port of Arabia, in Oman, situated on the Persian gulf, 230 m. NW of Muskat. It carries on a considerable trade with Arabia, Persia, and Scinde.

DEBA, or Dhaba, a town of Little Tibet, 250 m. SW of Lehdak, and 50 m. SW of Garoo, 6 m. W of the Sutledge, and 54 m. WNW of Rawan's Hrad, in N lat 31° 11', E long. 79° 55'. It is built on hills forming the side of a ravine, near which the Tiltil takes its course, and is sheltered on the N by lofty mountains. It consists of three parts,-the college or monastery which forms the residence of the lama and his gylums or monks, the convent, and the town properly so called in which the vizier resides. The houses are built of stone, two stories in height, and surmounted by a terrace. They present a neat whitewashed exterior, but within are destitute of cleanliness. In the centre of the town is a temple of Narayan (Vishnu), an irregular edifice, painted red, the door of which consists of copper, gilt and covered with grotesque figures, and surmounted by a small square building. The town contains also numerous minor temples, tombs of lamas, &c. In the rock on which the great temple stands are immense excavations, which the inhabitants use as granaries. Fine crops of grain called awa are grown in the neighbourhood, at an alt. of 15,000 ft. above sea-level.
DEBACH, a parish of Suffolk. Area 580 acres. Pop. 121.
DEBARKA, a town of Nubia, 110 m. ESE of Sennaar, near the r. bank of the Rahad.
DE BASTROP, a township of Chicot co., in the state of Arkansas, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 270.
DEBASSO, a town of Abyssinia, in the territory of the Dewaro, 75 m. E of Ankober.
DEBDEN, a parish of Essex, 4 m. NW of Thaxted, near the London and Cambridge railway. Area 5,040 acres. Pop. 979.
DEBDON, a township in the p. of Rothbury, Northumberland, 10 m. WSW of Alnwick, N of the Cocket. Pop. 13.

DEBENA, a town of Bambara, in Central Africa, on the route from Tangrera to Sausanding. It is composed of several small adjoining hamlets, is surrounded by a wall, and has a pop. of from 4,000 to 5,000.—Caillie.
DEBENHAM, a parish of Suffolk, 13 m. N of Ipswich, and 7 m. W of Framlington, at the source of the Deben, and near the London and Norwich railway. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 1,667.
DEBER, a town of Abyssinia, in the kingdom of Amhara, prov. of Dembia, on an affluent of the Angrab river, 30 m. NE of Gondar.
DEBESOS (SAN SEBASTIAN), a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. of Corunna, and partido of Santa Marta. Pop. 1,360.
DEBESY. See Debyosul.
DEBEYRA, or Debrous, a large village of Nubia, on the r. bank of the Nile, 90 m. SW of Dehr, and 18 m. below the cataract of Wady Halfa.
DEBIN, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, in the district of Kobail, 50 m. E of Chamir, and 60 m. NNW of Sana.
DEBLIN, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle and 17 m. NW of Brünn, and 8 m. ENE of Bilesch. On a height commanding this town are the ruins of an ancient fortress.

DE'BO (LAKE), a great lake of Central Africa, on the N frontier of the Banan country, and under the DEB buktu. Caillie states that, after a slow navigation of the majestic Dhioliba [Joliba], from a point 10 m. E of Jenne, and after passing some large islands which separate that river into several narrow branches of great depth, he reached this great lake; that land is seen on every side of the lake, except on the W, where it spreads out like an inland sea; that on coasting along its N side, and stretching WNW 15 m, he left a tongue of flat land on the l., which extends several miles to the S, and seems to close the passage of the lake, but divides it into two lakes, the upper and the lower; that the part through which the boat passed was calm and clear, with a depth of from 12 to 13 ft., had no perceptible current, extended in an E direction, and was surrounded by extensive marshes; that several high rocky islands appeared in it, and several large blocks of granite on the shore; and that the river on issuing from this part of the lake was about 6 m. broad. He adds, that when the Joliba is at its fullest, after the rains, the lake is much more extensive, and its banks are no longer perceptible.

DEBOD, or Debout, a village of Nubia, in a district of the same name, 15 m. SSW of Es-Souan, on the l. bank of the Nile, which here flows in a regular deep stream. It contains considerable ruins; and is supposed to occupy the place of the ancient Parenbole. The district extends along both sides of the Nile, under the parallel of 23° 50'.
DEB-RAJAH (COUNTRY OF). See BHOTAN.
